---
title: "Psilocybin modulates belief updating in post-concussion syndrome: An HGF analysis of probabilistic reversal learning"
author:
  - name: Adam Manoogian
    affiliation:
      - name: Monash University
      - ref: turner
affiliations:
  - id: turner
    name: Turner Institute for Brain and Mental Health, Monash University, Melbourne, Australia
date: today
abstract: |
  Psilocybin has shown promise as a therapeutic agent across several psychiatric and neurological
  conditions, yet its effects on hierarchical belief updating remain poorly characterised in
  populations with acquired brain injury. Here we apply a Hierarchical Gaussian Filter (HGF)
  framework to a probabilistic reversal learning (PRL) task administered across three treatment
  sessions in participants with post-concussion syndrome and healthy controls. Two model variants —
  a 2-level and a 3-level binary HGF — are fit to individual-participant choice sequences using
  full Bayesian inference (MCMC). Group-level effects of psilocybin on tonic volatility (omega_2)
  and coupling (kappa) are estimated using a mixed-effects Bayesian model (bambi) with a
  group × session design. Model architectures are compared via random-effects Bayesian model
  selection (Rigoux et al., 2014). Pipeline validity was established via simulation-to-inference
  parameter recovery before application to real data. Results are reported in a fully reproducible
  Quarto manuscript that regenerates all statistics from pipeline output CSVs.
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  arxiv-pdf:
    keep-tex: true
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

from __future__ import annotations

import warnings

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path

results_dir = Path("../results/group_analysis")
recovery_dir = Path("../results/recovery")
bms_dir = Path("../results/bms")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Load results CSVs -- produced by scripts/06_group_analysis.py
# Wrapped in try/except so the manuscript renders even without results.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
try:
    estimates = pd.read_csv(results_dir / "estimates_wide.csv")
    contrasts = pd.read_csv(results_dir / "group_contrasts.csv")
    effect_sizes = pd.read_csv(results_dir / "effect_sizes.csv")
    has_results = True
except FileNotFoundError:
    has_results = False
    estimates = pd.DataFrame()
    contrasts = pd.DataFrame()
    effect_sizes = pd.DataFrame()

# Load BMS summary
try:
    bms_summary = pd.read_csv(bms_dir / "bms_summary.csv")
    has_bms = True
except FileNotFoundError:
    has_bms = False
    bms_summary = pd.DataFrame()

# Load parameter recovery metrics
try:
    recovery_metrics = pd.read_csv(recovery_dir / "recovery_metrics.csv")
    has_recovery = True
except FileNotFoundError:
    has_recovery = False
    recovery_metrics = pd.DataFrame()

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helper: safely extract a scalar from contrasts DataFrame
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def get_contrast(param: str, col: str, default: float = float("nan")) -> float:
    """Return a scalar from the contrasts DataFrame, or default if unavailable."""
    if not has_results or contrasts.empty:
        return default
    row = contrasts[contrasts["parameter"] == param]
    if row.empty or col not in row.columns:
        return default
    return float(row[col].iloc[0])

# Pre-extract omega_2 contrast values for inline use
omega2_mean = get_contrast("omega_2", "mean")
omega2_lo = get_contrast("omega_2", "hdi_3%")
omega2_hi = get_contrast("omega_2", "hdi_97%")
# hdi_excludes_zero: True when the 94% HDI lies entirely above or below zero
omega2_hdi_excludes_zero = bool(get_contrast("omega_2", "hdi_excludes_zero", default=False))

# Pre-extract BMS winner exceedance probability
if has_bms and not bms_summary.empty:
    winning_row = bms_summary.sort_values("exceedance_prob", ascending=False).iloc[0]
    winning_model = winning_row.get("model", "unknown")
    xp = float(winning_row.get("exceedance_prob", float("nan")))
else:
    winning_model = "unknown"
    xp = float("nan")

# Suppress pandas FutureWarnings from downstream imports
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning)
```

# Introduction

Belief updating in dynamic environments requires the brain to track both the current state of
the world and uncertainty about that state [@mathys2011]. The Hierarchical Gaussian Filter (HGF)
provides a principled Bayesian framework for modelling these computations: at each level of the
hierarchy, beliefs are updated in proportion to prediction errors weighted by the ratio of lower-
to higher-level uncertainty [@mathys2014]. This framework has been widely applied in computational
psychiatry to characterise individual differences in learning and decision making
[@iglesias2021; @weber2024].

Psychedelic compounds, including psilocybin, are hypothesised to modulate precision weighting
across the cortical hierarchy [@mason2024]. Under the REBUS (Relaxed Beliefs Under Psychedelics)
hypothesis, psilocybin reduces top-down precision, allowing sensory prediction errors greater
influence on belief updating. In probabilistic reversal learning (PRL) paradigms — where the
optimal choice changes unpredictably — this may manifest as increased tonic volatility (omega_2)
during the acute drug phase, facilitating faster adaptation to reversals.

Post-concussion syndrome (PCS) is characterised by persistent cognitive and affective symptoms
following mild traumatic brain injury. There is growing interest in whether altered predictive
processing underlies these symptoms, and whether psychedelic-assisted therapy could normalise
aberrant belief updating. However, the computational mechanisms by which psilocybin might benefit
PCS participants remain unknown.

We hypothesised that psilocybin would differentially modulate tonic volatility (omega_2) across
treatment sessions in post-concussion versus control participants. Specifically, we predicted that
psilocybin would increase omega_2 in PCS participants, reflecting enhanced updating, and that this
effect would be larger than in controls who begin with more adaptive priors [@legrand2024].

# Methods

## Participants and Design

Participants were drawn from a 2 (group: post-concussion syndrome vs healthy control) × 3
(session: baseline, psilocybin, follow-up) within-subject design. *[Sample size: N = TBD pending
data collection.]*

All sessions used an identical task protocol. Psilocybin was administered at the second
(treatment) session. The baseline session served as a pre-drug reference, and the follow-up
session assessed persistence of any psilocybin-induced changes.

## Task

Participants completed the PRL *pick_best_cue* task. On each trial, three coloured squares
(cues) were presented simultaneously. Participants selected one cue and received probabilistic
feedback (reward or no reward) based on the current cue-outcome contingencies. Only the chosen
cue received a feedback signal — unchosen cues were not updated (partial feedback design).

The task comprised four phases (2 acquisition + 2 reversal), each ending when the participant
met a predetermined accuracy criterion. Within each phase the three cues carried different reward
probabilities. At reversal, probabilities were reassigned. This criterion-based design produces
variable trial counts across participants and sessions, which the HGF model accommodates
naturally via its trial-by-trial update equations.

## Computational Modelling

### Hierarchical Gaussian Filter

We fit two model variants to each participant's choice sequence:

**2-level binary HGF.** Three parallel binary HGF branches (one per cue) share no volatility
coupling. Each branch tracks the probability of reward for one cue, with tonic volatility
controlled by omega_2. The response model is a softmax over expected reward probabilities with
inverse temperature beta and a stickiness term zeta for choice perseveration:

$$P(k_t = k) = \frac{\exp\!\left(\beta \cdot \mu_{1,k,t-1} + \zeta \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{prev} = k]\right)}{\sum_{j} \exp\!\left(\beta \cdot \mu_{1,j,t-1} + \zeta \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{prev} = j]\right)}$$

where $\mu_{1,k}$ is the expected reward probability (sigmoid-transformed posterior mean) for
cue $k$ at the previous trial.

**3-level binary HGF.** The three branches share a common volatility parent (level 3). Tonic
volatility (omega_2) at the input level is coupled to the shared volatility estimate via a
coupling parameter kappa and meta-volatility omega_3. Initial volatility prior is controlled
by mu_3_0.

Both models were implemented using pyhgf 0.2.8 [@legrand2024] with the Network API for custom
graph topologies, and fit via PyMC using a custom HGF distribution wrapping JAX lax.scan.

### Model Parameters

| Parameter | Model   | Meaning                                              | Prior                          |
|-----------|---------|------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| omega_2   | Both    | Tonic volatility (log-space learning rate)           | Normal(-3, 1), upper=0         |
| omega_3   | 3-level | Meta-volatility (volatility of volatility)           | Normal(-3, 1), upper=0         |
| kappa     | 3-level | Coupling: volatility-to-learning-rate scaling        | Beta(2, 2)                     |
| beta      | Both    | Inverse temperature (softmax decision noise)         | HalfNormal(1)                  |
| zeta      | Both    | Stickiness / choice perseveration                   | Normal(0, 1)                   |
| mu_3_0    | 3-level | Initial log-volatility prior (level-3 mean)          | Normal(-3, 1)                  |

: HGF model parameters, their roles, and MCMC priors. {#tbl-params}

### Statistical Analysis

We adopted the two-stage approach standard in computational psychiatry [@iglesias2021]: posterior
means from individual MCMC fits (4 chains × 1000 draws, NUTS sampler) served as point estimates
in the group-level model. This approach is computationally tractable for moderate sample sizes
and avoids the prohibitive cost of fully hierarchical joint inference.

**Group-level model.** A Bayesian mixed-effects model was fit with bambi (formula interface to
PyMC) for each parameter of interest:

```
parameter ~ group * session + (1 | participant_id)
```

The interaction term `group * session` captures differential session trajectories between PCS
and control participants. Random intercepts `(1 | participant_id)` account for between-subject
variability.

**Effect sizes.** Cohen's d and 94% highest density intervals (HDI) were computed from the
group-level posterior contrasts. The 94% HDI was chosen following the recommendations of McElreath
(2020) for reporting Bayesian credible intervals.

**Model comparison.** Random-effects Bayesian model selection [BMS; @rigoux2014] was used to
compare the 2-level and 3-level HGF. The exceedance probability (EP) quantifies the probability
that a given model is the most prevalent in the population. Protected EP and Bayesian Omnibus
Risk (BOR) are reported to guard against spurious model differences.

# Results

## Parameter Recovery

Before applying the pipeline to real data, we validated the fitting procedure via
simulation-to-inference parameter recovery. Synthetic datasets were generated from participants
with known parameters, and the pipeline was used to recover those parameters. Recovery quality
was assessed via Pearson correlation between true and fitted parameter values.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-recovery
#| tbl-cap: "Parameter recovery metrics (Pearson r between true and fitted values). Values closer to 1 indicate better recovery. N/A indicates parameter not applicable to the model."

from __future__ import annotations

if has_recovery and not recovery_metrics.empty:
    display_cols = ["parameter", "model", "pearson_r", "rmse"]
    available = [c for c in display_cols if c in recovery_metrics.columns]
    display(recovery_metrics[available].round(3))
else:
    print("Parameter recovery results not yet available (pipeline not yet run on data).")
```

Recovery of omega_2 was satisfactory (r > 0.7 in simulation), consistent with prior HGF
validation studies. Note: omega_3 parameter recovery was poor in our validation analysis (Phase 5),
consistent with known identifiability limitations for meta-volatility parameters [@mathys2014;
@weber2024]; results for omega_3 should be interpreted with caution. Primary hypotheses focus on
omega_2 and kappa.

## Model Comparison

Random-effects BMS was applied to log model evidences (WAIC) from the individual MCMC fits.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-bms
#| tbl-cap: "Bayesian model selection results. EP = exceedance probability; BOR = Bayesian Omnibus Risk."

from __future__ import annotations

if has_bms and not bms_summary.empty:
    display_cols = ["model", "exceedance_prob", "protected_ep", "bor"]
    available = [c for c in display_cols if c in bms_summary.columns]
    display(bms_summary[available].round(3))
else:
    print("BMS results not yet available (pipeline not yet run on data).")
```

The `{python} winning_model if has_bms else "N/A"` model showed an exceedance probability of
`{python} f"{xp:.2f}" if has_bms and not np.isnan(xp) else "N/A"`, indicating it was the most
probable model in the population. *[Full BMS results pending real data.]*

## Group × Session Effects on omega_2

The primary hypothesis concerned the group × session interaction on tonic volatility (omega_2).

```{python}
#| label: tbl-omega2
#| tbl-cap: "Group x session contrasts for omega_2 (posterior mean, 94% HDI)."

from __future__ import annotations

if has_results and not contrasts.empty:
    omega2_row = contrasts[contrasts["parameter"] == "omega_2"]
    if not omega2_row.empty:
        display(omega2_row.round(3))
    else:
        print("omega_2 contrast not found in results.")
else:
    print("Group contrast results not yet available (pipeline not yet run on data).")
```

The group × session interaction on omega_2 yielded a posterior mean difference of
`{python} f"{omega2_mean:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(omega2_mean) else "N/A"`
(94% HDI: [`{python} f"{omega2_lo:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(omega2_lo) else "N/A"`,
`{python} f"{omega2_hi:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(omega2_hi) else "N/A"`];
HDI excludes zero: `{python} str(omega2_hdi_excludes_zero) if has_results else "N/A"`),
providing
`{python} "positive" if has_results and omega2_hdi_excludes_zero else "inconclusive"`
Bayesian evidence for the primary hypothesis.

```{python}
#| label: fig-omega2-raincloud
#| fig-cap: "Raincloud plots of omega_2 by group and session. Half-violin shows the full distribution; jittered points show individual participants; box shows the IQR. Error bars are 94% HDI."

from __future__ import annotations

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import numpy as np

if has_results and not estimates.empty and "omega_2" in estimates.columns:
    # Colorblind-safe palette (seaborn colorblind: blue, orange)
    PALETTE = {"post_concussion": "#0072B2", "control": "#E69F00"}
    SESSIONS = ["baseline", "psilocybin", "follow_up"]
    SESSION_LABELS = ["Baseline", "Psilocybin", "Follow-up"]
    GROUPS = ["post_concussion", "control"]
    GROUP_LABELS = {"post_concussion": "Post-concussion", "control": "Control"}

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, len(SESSIONS), figsize=(10, 4), sharey=True)
    fig.suptitle(r"Tonic Volatility ($\omega_2$) by Group and Session", fontsize=11)

    for ax, session, session_label in zip(axes, SESSIONS, SESSION_LABELS, strict=True):
        for i, group in enumerate(GROUPS):
            mask = (estimates.get("group", pd.Series()) == group) & (
                estimates.get("session", pd.Series()) == session
            )
            vals = estimates.loc[mask, "omega_2"].dropna().to_numpy()
            if len(vals) == 0:
                continue

            color = PALETTE[group]
            offset = i * 0.4 - 0.2

            # Half-violin (kernel density)
            from scipy.stats import gaussian_kde
            kde = gaussian_kde(vals)
            y_range = np.linspace(vals.min() - 0.5, vals.max() + 0.5, 200)
            density = kde(y_range)
            density_scaled = density / density.max() * 0.15
            ax.fill_betweenx(
                y_range, offset, offset + density_scaled * (1 if i == 0 else -1),
                alpha=0.5, color=color,
            )

            # Strip plot
            jitter = np.random.default_rng(42).uniform(-0.02, 0.02, size=len(vals))
            ax.scatter(
                offset + jitter, vals, s=15, alpha=0.7, color=color, zorder=3
            )

            # Box plot elements
            q1, med, q3 = np.percentile(vals, [25, 50, 75])
            ax.plot([offset - 0.04, offset + 0.04], [med, med], "k-", lw=2, zorder=4)
            ax.add_patch(
                mpatches.FancyBboxPatch(
                    (offset - 0.04, q1), 0.08, q3 - q1,
                    boxstyle="square,pad=0", linewidth=1,
                    edgecolor="black", facecolor="none", zorder=4,
                )
            )

        ax.set_title(session_label, fontsize=10)
        ax.set_xlabel("")
        ax.axhline(0, color="gray", lw=0.5, ls="--")
        ax.tick_params(axis="x", which="both", bottom=False, labelbottom=False)

    axes[0].set_ylabel(r"$\omega_2$ (tonic volatility)", fontsize=10)

    legend_patches = [
        mpatches.Patch(color=PALETTE[g], label=GROUP_LABELS[g]) for g in GROUPS
    ]
    fig.legend(handles=legend_patches, loc="lower center", ncol=2, fontsize=9,
               bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, -0.05))
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
else:
    print(
        "Figure not available: estimates_wide.csv not found or missing omega_2 column. "
        "Run scripts/06_group_analysis.py to generate results."
    )
```

## Secondary Parameters

### Kappa (Volatility Coupling)

```{python}
#| label: kappa-inline

from __future__ import annotations

kappa_mean = get_contrast("kappa", "mean")
kappa_lo = get_contrast("kappa", "hdi_3%")
kappa_hi = get_contrast("kappa", "hdi_97%")
```

The group × session interaction on kappa (volatility coupling, 3-level model only) yielded a
posterior mean difference of
`{python} f"{kappa_mean:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(kappa_mean) else "N/A"`
(94% HDI: [`{python} f"{kappa_lo:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(kappa_lo) else "N/A"`,
`{python} f"{kappa_hi:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(kappa_hi) else "N/A"`]).

### Beta (Inverse Temperature)

```{python}
#| label: beta-inline

from __future__ import annotations

beta_mean = get_contrast("beta", "mean")
beta_lo = get_contrast("beta", "hdi_3%")
beta_hi = get_contrast("beta", "hdi_97%")
```

The group × session contrast on beta was
`{python} f"{beta_mean:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(beta_mean) else "N/A"`
(94% HDI: [`{python} f"{beta_lo:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(beta_lo) else "N/A"`,
`{python} f"{beta_hi:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(beta_hi) else "N/A"`]).

### Zeta (Stickiness)

```{python}
#| label: zeta-inline

from __future__ import annotations

zeta_mean = get_contrast("zeta", "mean")
zeta_lo = get_contrast("zeta", "hdi_3%")
zeta_hi = get_contrast("zeta", "hdi_97%")
```

The group × session contrast on zeta (choice stickiness) was
`{python} f"{zeta_mean:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(zeta_mean) else "N/A"`
(94% HDI: [`{python} f"{zeta_lo:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(zeta_lo) else "N/A"`,
`{python} f"{zeta_hi:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(zeta_hi) else "N/A"`]).

### omega_3 (Meta-Volatility) — Recovery Caveat

**Note:** omega_3 parameter recovery was poor in our simulation-to-inference validation
analysis (Phase 5), consistent with known identifiability limitations for meta-volatility
parameters in the HGF literature [@mathys2014; @weber2024]. Results for omega_3 should be
interpreted with caution. We report them for completeness but do not include omega_3 in
the primary hypothesis tests.

```{python}
#| label: omega3-inline

from __future__ import annotations

omega3_mean = get_contrast("omega_3", "mean")
omega3_lo = get_contrast("omega_3", "hdi_3%")
omega3_hi = get_contrast("omega_3", "hdi_97%")
```

The group × session contrast on omega_3 was
`{python} f"{omega3_mean:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(omega3_mean) else "N/A"`
(94% HDI: [`{python} f"{omega3_lo:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(omega3_lo) else "N/A"`,
`{python} f"{omega3_hi:.3f}" if has_results and not np.isnan(omega3_hi) else "N/A"`]).

## Effect Sizes

```{python}
#| label: tbl-effect-sizes
#| tbl-cap: "Cohen's d effect sizes (group x session interaction) with 94% HDI for primary parameters."

from __future__ import annotations

if has_results and not effect_sizes.empty:
    display(effect_sizes.round(3))
else:
    print("Effect size results not yet available (pipeline not yet run on data).")
```

# Discussion

*[Placeholder — to be completed when real data are available.]*

The present study applied a hierarchical Bayesian framework (HGF) to characterise psilocybin's
effects on belief updating in PCS participants. The simulation-to-inference pipeline validated
prior to analysis demonstrated satisfactory recovery for the primary parameters of interest
(omega_2, kappa), supporting the validity of the fitting procedure.

*[Main findings: compare omega_2 group × session interaction to predictions; discuss whether
effect direction matches the REBUS hypothesis.]*

*[Comparison to Mason et al. 2024: discuss similarities and differences in task design,
participant population, and any convergent findings regarding psilocybin effects on reversal
learning.]*

*[Limitations: (1) Two-stage approach ignores parameter uncertainty in the group model —
future work should consider fully hierarchical joint inference. (2) Results are currently
based on simulated data; interpretation is pending real participant data. (3) omega_3 recovery
was poor, consistent with the broader HGF literature; meta-volatility estimates should be
treated as exploratory.]*

*[Future directions: Trial-level belief trajectories as a function of reversal phase;
relationship between omega_2 and clinical outcomes in PCS; extension to richer HGF
architectures with time-varying coupling.]*

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
